FY2022 purchases of property, plant and equipment were $1,749 million versus net cash provided by operating activities of $5,591 million (free cash flow conversion 66%). The document does not explicitly state that 3M is capital‑intensive, though these figures show meaningful capital spending in 2022.

3M’s FY2022 operating margin decline was driven chiefly by a 2.4 percentage-point raw material and logistics cost headwind, foreign exchange translation impacts (decreasing operating income by approximately $271 million), lost income and stranded costs from divestitures (including the Food Safety split‑off and Aearo deconsolidation), and special items such as net litigation costs and PFAS manufacturing exit costs, partially offset by organic growth/productivity (benefits from strong pricing, spending discipline and prior restructuring) which contributed roughly +1.0–1.3 percentage points (with a −0.3pp impact from declines in disposable respirator demand).  
As a result, reported operating margin fell to 19.1% in 2022 from 20.8% in 2021.
Consumer segment.

3M’s quick ratio for Q2 FY2023 using cash (4,258) + current marketable securities (56) + accounts receivable, net (4,947) = 9,261; quick ratio = 9,261 / 10,936 ≈ 0.85. Because it is below 1, quick assets are insufficient to cover current liabilities, indicating the liquidity profile is not reasonably healthy.
None.

Yes; cash dividends declared and paid totaled $1.50 and $1.49 per share for the first and second quarters of 2023 and 2022, respectively, or $3.00 and $2.98 per share for the first six months of 2023 and 2022, respectively.

No — Adobe’s operating margin fell to about 34.6% in fiscal 2022 (operating income $6,098M on revenue $17,606M) from about 36.8% in fiscal 2021, though it remained above the roughly 32.9% reported in fiscal 2020.
The document does not disclose Free cash flow conversion.

Conventional inventory turnover for FY2022 is not meaningful for AES because the Company primarily generates and sells electricity under long‑term and short‑term contracts rather than selling physical inventory. Many generation contracts recover fuel as pass‑throughs and 46% of AES’ generation capacity is renewables with no fuel costs, so the typical “times sold” inventory metric does not apply.

The 8‑K reported that Amcor Finance (USA), Inc. and Amcor Flexibles North America, Inc. entered into supplemental indentures to effect the substitution of Amcor Flexibles North America, Inc. as issuer for Amcor Finance (USA), Inc. and the assumption by the Substitute Issuer of the former issuer’s covenants under the indentures governing the 3.625% Guaranteed Senior Notes due 2026 and the 4.500% Guaranteed Senior Notes due 2028.

The quick ratio is not reported in this document and cannot be determined for FY2023 versus FY2022 because the consolidated current asset and current liability details (and inventories) for both years required to calculate the quick ratio are not provided. The document only includes combined Obligor Group current assets and current liabilities for June 30, 2023 and does not provide the comparable FY2022 figures needed for a comparison.

FY2023: On August 1, 2022, Amcor completed the acquisition of a Czech Republic flexible packaging manufacturing plant; on March 17, 2023, it completed the acquisition of a medical device packaging manufacturing site in Shanghai, China; and on May 31, 2023, it completed the acquisition of a New Zealand–based manufacturer of automated protein packaging machines. FY2022 and FY2021: the document does not report any major acquisitions in those fiscal years.
Amcor primarily operates in the packaging industry.

No — Amcor's gross profit decreased by $95 million in fiscal year 2023, so gross margin did not improve, although operating income as a percentage of net sales rose to 10.3% from 8.5% in FY2022. Gross-margin analysis is further distorted by $776 million of pass-through raw material costs and a $215 million pre-tax net gain on disposal of the Russian business, so gross margin may be a less useful metric for underlying performance.

AMCOR's restructuring liability at December 31, 2022 is a $93 million accrual consisting of employee costs ($81m), fixed-asset related costs ($2m) and other costs ($10m) arising from restructuring activities (primarily related to the Russia‑Ukraine conflict and prior Bemis integration) and presented as restructuring and other related activities, net. The accruals are recorded under other current and other non‑current liabilities and are intended to cover payments for those restructuring‑related expenses, with the majority expected to be paid within the next twelve months.
Adjusted EBITDA for the twelve months ended June 30, 2023 was $2,018 million.

The document does not state a quick ratio for FY22; instead it describes AMD’s liquidity as relying on "cash, cash equivalents and short-term investments together with the availability under ... the Revolving Credit Agreement, our commercial paper program, and our cash flows from operations," so the quick ratio alone is not the most relevant measure because it would not reflect those short-term investments and committed credit sources.

AMD’s major products and services include client processors (AMD Ryzen, AMD Ryzen PRO, Ryzen Threadripper, Threadripper PRO, AMD Athlon, Athlon PRO and AMD PRO A‑Series), consumer and professional graphics (AMD Radeon, AMD Radeon PRO), data‑center graphics and accelerators (Radeon Instinct, Radeon PRO V‑series, AMD Instinct), server microprocessors (AMD EPYC), chipsets, semi‑custom SoC products and related engineering services, embedded processors and APUs (Embedded EPYC, Embedded Ryzen V‑Series, R‑Series, G‑Series, Geode), FPGAs and Adaptive SoCs and development tools (Virtex, Kintex, Artix, Spartan families; Zynq, Zynq UltraScale+, Versal, Vitis, Vivado) and compute and network acceleration boards (Alveo).

Revenue change in fiscal 2022 was driven by a 64% increase in Data Center sales primarily from higher EPYC server processor sales, a 21% increase in Gaming driven by higher semi‑custom gaming console SoC sales (partially offset by lower gaming graphics shipments), a significant Embedded increase primarily from inclusion of Xilinx embedded product revenue after the acquisition, and a 10% decline in Client revenue due to a 24% decrease in unit shipments partially offset by a 19% increase in average selling price.

Operating margin declined in FY2022 primarily due to amortization of intangible assets associated with the Xilinx acquisition, which reduced gross margin (45% in 2022 vs. 48% in 2021) and drove operating income down to $1.3 billion from $3.6 billion.
Operating activities; net cash provided by operating activities was $3,565 million in FY22.

United States; the United Kingdom; the European Union; Australia; Japan; Canada; and Mexico.

Operating margin is not a useful metric for a company like American Express because, as a card-issuing financial services company, its performance is driven by net interest income, provisions for credit losses and related yields rather than a traditional operating‑profit line. The filing therefore emphasizes metrics such as net interest yield on average Card Member loans (10.6% in 2022), return on average equity (32.3% in 2022) and total revenues net of interest expense ($52,862 million) with provisions ($2,182 million) and expenses ($41,095 million) shown separately.

Gross margin is not a useful metric for American Express because it is a globally integrated payments and banking company whose primary revenues are discount revenue (merchant fees), interest income, net card fees, service fees and processed revenue rather than product sales where gross margin typically applies. The document highlights discount revenue as the largest revenue source and focuses on metrics such as discount revenue, net interest income/net interest yield and provisions for credit losses that are more relevant to its business model.
It decreased by 3.0 percentage points (from 24.6% in FY2021 to 21.6% in FY2022).

No — as of December 31, 2022 current assets were $1,250 million and current liabilities were $2,811 million, yielding negative working capital of $1,561 million. Working capital is of limited usefulness for American Water because the Company’s current liabilities may exceed current assets mainly from debt maturities due within one year and the use of short-term debt to meet scheduled maturities, fund acquisitions and construction projects, and address seasonal cash needs.

The document contains no gross margin figures or discussion, so it does not permit assessing whether Best Buy’s gross margins have historically been consistent within ~2% year-to-year. The filing instead highlights factors—rapid technological and product life‑cycle changes, heavy competitive promotional activity, concentration of revenue in the fiscal fourth quarter, and fixed or multi‑year operating costs and promotional financing impacts—that can cause pricing and profitability variability, meaning gross margins may be volatile or not fully informative on their own for assessing Best Buy’s performance.

In fiscal 2022, Best Buy acquired all of the outstanding shares of Current Health Ltd. (“Current Health”) and Two Peaks, LLC d/b/a Yardbird Furniture (“Yardbird”). The document does not disclose major acquisitions for fiscal 2023 or fiscal 2021.
Operating activities, which provided $1,824 million in fiscal 2023.

Yes; Commercial Airplanes, Defense, Space & Security, and Global Services each represented more than 20% of Boeing’s FY2022 revenues.

Yes — Item 3 states Boeing is “involved in a number of legal proceedings” and Note 21 discusses legal proceedings resulting from the October 29, 2018 Lion Air Flight 610 and the March 10, 2019 Ethiopian Airlines Flight 302 accidents.

Gross margin is not a useful metric for a company like Boeing. Boeing uses program accounting—inventoriable production costs are accumulated and charged as cost of sales by program and the program accounting quantity is a key determinant of the gross margins recognized on sales of individual airplanes, and because those margins depend on significant judgments and estimates (which could materially change revenues and profit amounts), gross margin can be misleading.
Commercial airlines worldwide and government customers—primarily the U.S. Department of Defense (and other U.S. government agencies and non‑U.S. customers, including foreign military sales).
Yes — the commercial aircraft market and the airline industry have historically been cyclical, which significantly impacts demand for Boeing’s commercial aircraft and related services.

Boeing expects the 787 program to gradually increase to 5 per month in 2023, to resume 777X production in 2023 (production is currently paused), and to gradually increase 737 production rates above the current 31 per month based on market demand and supply chain capacity.
Boeing's effective tax rate was (0.6)% in FY2022 compared with 14.7% in FY2021.
